blocks.headline: Specialist Assessments That Explain What's Going On and How to Help
blocks.subtitle: Expert EPs assessing autism, dyslexia, ADHD, and other learning difficulties. Clear diagnosis, practical strategies, classroom guidance. Schools and LAs nationwide.

blocks.heading: Specialist Assessment Services
blocks.subheading: Comprehensive assessment and diagnosis for the most common specific learning difficulties and developmental conditions.

cards.title: Autism Spectrum Assessment
cards.description: Comprehensive assessment for autism in children and young people. Diagnostic clarity, strengths profile, support needs identified. Practical strategies for home and school.

cards.title: Dyslexia & Specific Learning Difficulties
cards.description: Detailed assessment of dyslexia, dyscalculia, dysgraphia. Understanding of processing strengths and weaknesses. Exam access arrangements support.

cards.title: ADHD Assessment
cards.description: Educational perspective on ADHD presentation, impact on learning and behaviour, strategies for focus and organisation. Complements medical diagnosis.

cards.title: Language & Communication Assessment
cards.description: Assessment of speech, language, and communication needs. How communication difficulties affect learning. Recommendations for support and intervention.

cards.title: Social-Emotional Assessment
cards.description: Understanding of emotional development, resilience, friendship difficulties, social anxiety. Strategies for emotional support.

cards.title: Attachment & Relationship Assessment
cards.description: Assessment of attachment patterns, relationship difficulties, or trauma effects. Recommendations for therapeutic and relational support.

blocks.heading: Understanding Key Conditions and When Assessment Helps

tabs.label: Autism Spectrum Disorder (ASD)
tabs.content:
### What is autism?

Autism is a lifelong neurodevelopmental difference affecting how a person perceives the world and interacts socially. It is not a deficit but a difference. Many autistic people have significant strengths (visual thinking, pattern recognition, focused interests, reliability) alongside areas requiring support (social communication, sensory sensitivity, change management).

### Why assessment matters

- Diagnostic clarity — understanding whether the child is autistic
- Strengths identification — knowing what the child is good at
- Support needs clarification — what accommodations and strategies help
- School planning — how to adapt the environment and teaching
- Identity and acceptance — helping the child understand themselves
- Exam access arrangements — if needed, evidence for reasonable adjustments

### What the assessment includes

- Developmental history and background
- Observation of social communication, play, interaction
- Structured interviews with parents and teachers
- Testing of cognitive ability and processing style
- Sensory profile assessment
- Functional impact analysis
- Recommendations for school support and strategies

tabs.label: Dyslexia and Specific Learning Difficulties (SpLD)
tabs.content:
### What is dyslexia?

Dyslexia is a specific learning difficulty affecting how the brain processes written and/or spoken language. It's not about intelligence but about how language processing happens. Common presentations include difficulty with reading fluency, spelling, writing speed, or phonological awareness.

### Why assessment matters

- Diagnosis — confirmation that dyslexia is present, not just poor literacy
- Processing profile — understanding the child's specific language-processing strengths and weaknesses
- Early intervention — knowing what teaching methods work best for this child
- Exam access arrangements — evidence for reader, scribe, extra time
- Self-understanding — helping the child understand why literacy is harder for them
- School strategies — tailored classroom support and alternative assessment methods

### What the assessment includes

- Cognitive ability testing
- Literacy and phonological awareness testing
- Speed of processing testing
- Reading, spelling, and writing samples
- History of educational progress
- Functional impact on learning
- Recommendations for school and exam support

tabs.label: ADHD (Educational Psychology Perspective)
tabs.content:
### What is ADHD?

ADHD affects executive function: the ability to sustain attention, inhibit impulses, organise, plan, and manage time. Medical diagnosis is made by a doctor. Educational Psychologists assess the impact on learning and school behaviour, and recommend classroom and study strategies.

### Why educational assessment matters

- Understanding the learning impact — how ADHD affects focus, organisation, and academic performance
- School strategies — what classroom adjustments and support help this child succeed
- Exam access — evidence for separate invigilation, time management support, breaks
- Self-understanding — helping the child recognise their own attention patterns and develop strategies
- Complementing medical diagnosis — providing the school perspective alongside medical diagnosis

### What the assessment includes

- Cognitive ability and processing speed testing
- Attention and executive function assessment
- Behaviour rating from parents and teachers
- Classroom observation
- Work samples and learning history
- Impact on academic performance and behaviour
- Strategies for focus, organisation, and time management

blocks.heading: What to Expect from a Specialist Assessment

steps.title: Initial Consultation
steps.description: We discuss your concerns, what you've noticed, and what you hope to understand. No obligation — just a conversation to clarify if assessment is appropriate.

steps.title: Full Assessment
steps.description: The specialist EP conducts a thorough assessment (2–4 hours across 1–3 visits). Testing, observation, interviews with parents/teachers, and review of school work.

steps.title: Analysis & Report
steps.description: The EP analyses findings, writes the report, and quality-checks it. Typically delivered within 2 weeks.

steps.title: Next Steps
steps.description: The report includes diagnosis if appropriate, a profile of strengths and needs, and practical strategies. School and family can implement recommendations immediately.

blocks.heading: Why Get a Specialist Educational Psychology Assessment?

rows.col2: Limited to classroom observation or medical perspective only
Not available from teacher observation or medical diagnosis
General advice only or limited to medical treatment
Not formal evidence; usually insufficient alone
General observation; not focus of medical assessment
Piecemeal over time; medical diagnosis + separate Ed Psych = more expensive

caseStudies.title: Autism Assessment Led to Whole-School Understanding
caseStudies.summary: A secondary student with undiagnosed autism was struggling with social relationships and sensory overwhelm. An educational psychology assessment provided diagnostic clarity and led to school adjustments that helped the student thrive.

caseStudies.title: Dyslexia Identification Unlocked Literacy Support
caseStudies.summary: A primary school child was falling behind in literacy despite effort. An educational psychology assessment confirmed dyslexia and identified the child's strengths (visual-spatial, practical). Targeted intervention followed.

caseStudies.title: ADHD Support Improved Classroom Behaviour and Engagement
caseStudies.summary: A child was struggling with focus and impulsive behaviour. A specialist educational psychology assessment clarified ADHD patterns and provided the teacher with evidence-based strategies for supporting focus and self-regulation.

faqs.question: What's the difference between an educational psychology assessment and a medical diagnosis?
faqs.answer: A medical diagnosis (e.g., autism, ADHD from a doctor) confirms the condition. An educational psychology assessment provides the school-based perspective: how the condition affects learning, what classroom support helps, and what exam adjustments are appropriate. Both are valuable; they answer different questions.

faqs.question: Can an educational psychologist diagnose autism or ADHD?
faqs.answer: Educational Psychologists assess and provide educational perspectives on autism and ADHD. Formal medical diagnosis is made by a doctor (GP or psychiatrist). Often both assessments are helpful â€” medical diagnosis confirms the condition, educational psychology assessment informs school support.

faqs.question: How long does a specialist assessment take?
faqs.answer: The assessment itself typically takes 2â€“4 hours across 1â€“3 visits depending on the child and condition. The full report, including analysis and write-up, usually takes 2 weeks. From referral to receiving the report, expect 3â€“4 weeks.

faqs.question: How much does a specialist assessment cost?
faqs.answer: Costs vary by assessment type and complexity. Typically ÂŁ900â€“1,500. We always provide a fixed quotation upfront. For schools and LAs, volume discounts may be available.

faqs.question: Will the school accept the assessment report?
faqs.answer: Yes. Educational Psychology reports are recognised by schools and local authorities as evidence for support planning, exam access arrangements, and EHCP assessments. Schools accept reports from HCPC-registered Educational Psychologists.

faqs.question: Can I get a specialist assessment if my child's teacher hasn't referred them?
faqs.answer: Assessments commissioned by schools go through the school. If a parent suspects their child needs assessment, they should speak to their school SENCO. We work with schools and LAs, not directly with private families.

faqs.question: What if the assessment doesn't show a difficulty? What's the point?
faqs.answer: Assessments can clarify that a difficulty is not present â€” which is valuable information. Or they can identify other factors affecting learning (anxiety, processing differences, learning styles) that weren't previously clear. Assessment informs what support is actually needed.

faqs.question: Can we get an assessment if the child has never had a diagnosis?
faqs.answer: Absolutely. An assessment can identify whether a condition like autism or dyslexia is present, even without a previous diagnosis. This is often the first step toward understanding and supporting a child.

faqs.question: What happens after the assessment report?
faqs.answer: The school uses the report to plan support. Depending on the findings, the school might implement classroom strategies, provide targeted intervention, apply for exam access arrangements, or begin EHCP assessment if needs are more complex. The EP is available for follow-up consultation.

faqs.question: Can we use the assessment report for exam access arrangements?
faqs.answer: Yes, if the assessment identifies needs for exam access arrangements (e.g., dyslexia â†’ extra time/reader; ADHD â†’ separate invigilation). The report provides the formal evidence for the exam board.

faqs.question: How long is the assessment report?
faqs.answer: Typically 10â€“20 pages depending on the assessment type and complexity. Written for multiple audiences: schools, parents, and exam boards. Clear language, practical recommendations, and actionable strategies included.

blocks.heading: Unsure If Your Child Needs a Specialist Assessment?
blocks.bodyText: Talk to your school SENCO. If you think an assessment might help, we're here to provide thorough, practical expertise. Clear diagnosis, practical strategies, clear next steps.
